questions.question: How should you handle this generated code security concern?
questions.scenario: Claude Code generates a database query function that constructs SQL using string concatenation with user-provided values: query = f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = "{user_email}"'. Your security review identifies this as a SQL injection vulnerability. The CLAUDE.md does not mention SQL injection prevention.
options: Review all existing Claude Code generated database code for the same vulnerability pattern
Add a security section to CLAUDE.md specifying that all database queries must use parameterized queries or ORM methods, with explicit examples showing the correct pattern
Raise this as a bug report to the Claude Code team since it shouldn't generate insecure patterns
Add a post-generation security scanner to the CI pipeline that catches SQL injection patterns before merging
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Documenting the parameterized query requirement in CLAUDE.md prevents future occurrences systematically — the root cause is missing security standards, not a one-time error.
[Why B fails]: A scanner catches the symptom but doesn't prevent it — Claude Code will keep generating the pattern until the standard is documented.
[Why C fails]: Auditing existing code is necessary but secondary — first prevent future occurrences, then audit historical ones.
[Why D fails]: Claude Code generates code based on patterns and standards provided — the gap is in the project's CLAUDE.md, not a bug in the tool.
[Principle]: Document security requirements in CLAUDE.md to prevent future insecure patterns rather than just catching them after generation.
[Doc]: CLAUDE.md & Memory → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory
[Doc]: CLAUDE.md in SDK → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/claude-code-features
[Doc]: Secure Deployment Guide → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/secure-deployment
[Doc]: Security Overview → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/security

questions.question: What retrieval strategy best handles this multi-document query?
questions.scenario: Your enterprise knowledge base has 50,000 documents. An employee asks: 'What is our current policy on remote work expenses and how does it interact with our travel policy?' Semantic search returns 12 potentially relevant documents. The answer requires synthesizing information from: the remote work policy (doc 3), the expense reimbursement policy (doc 7), and the travel policy (doc 11). However, the top-ranked retrieval result is an outdated version of the expense policy from 2021.
options: Implement a re-ranker model that scores the 12 documents for relevance before passing the top 3 to synthesis
Pass all 12 retrieved documents to the synthesis prompt and instruct the model to use the most recent document when versions conflict
Implement metadata-filtered retrieval that prioritizes the most recently dated policy version per document type, then pass only the current versions (docs 3, 7, 11) to the synthesis prompt
Ask the user to specify which version of the expense policy they want to reference before proceeding
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Metadata-filtered retrieval (latest version per document type) prevents stale policy versions from contaminating the synthesis, which is the primary failure mode here.
[Why B fails]: Passing all 12 including the outdated version and hoping the model selects the right one is unreliable — models don't consistently apply 'use most recent' instructions when confronted with conflicting versions.
[Why C fails]: A re-ranker improves relevance but doesn't specifically address version currency — an outdated document may still rank highly for relevance while being incorrect.
[Why D fails]: Asking users to specify versions adds friction and assumes employees know which version is current — the system should handle this transparently.
[Principle]: Filter retrieved documents by metadata (recency, version) to prevent stale information from entering synthesis, rather than relying on the model to select correctly.
[Doc]: RAG (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: What citation design best supports verifiability?
questions.scenario: Your enterprise RAG system answers questions about company policies. Employees need to be able to verify the answers against source documents. Currently, the system provides accurate answers but employees have no way to trace which policy document or section the answer came from. Compliance audits require source traceability.
options: Add a confidence score to the response (e.g., '92% confidence') so employees can judge whether to verify independently
Add a generic statement at the end of every response: 'Based on internal policy documents. Contact HR to verify.'
Include the full text of all retrieved documents as an appendix to the response so employees can verify by reading them
For each claim in the response, include an inline citation with: document name, section, and last-updated date — and provide direct document links in a references section
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Inline claim-level citations with document, section, date, and direct links provide the traceability compliance audits require and employees need for verification.
[Why B fails]: 'Based on internal policy documents' is a generic disclaimer, not a traceable citation — it satisfies neither employee verification nor audit requirements.
[Why C fails]: Including full document text bloats responses and makes them unusable; targeted citations are more useful and sufficient for verification.
[Why D fails]: Confidence scores indicate reliability but don't provide the source traceability needed for compliance — auditors need to see the exact source, not a probability.
[Principle]: Provide claim-level citations with specific document, section, and date — not generic disclaimers or confidence scores.
[Doc]: Citations (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/citations
[Doc]: Compliance & Monitoring → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/monitoring-usage

questions.question: How should the system handle this knowledge gap?
questions.scenario: An employee asks your enterprise RAG system: 'What is our maternity leave policy for contract employees?' The knowledge base contains the maternity leave policy for full-time employees (clearly documented) but has no document covering contract employee benefits. Semantic search returns the full-time policy as the top result with 0.78 similarity.
options: Synthesize an answer by combining the full-time maternity leave policy with general contract employment principles from other documents
Answer with the full-time employee policy since it's the most relevant result, with a note that policies may differ for contract employees

Clearly state that the knowledge base contains the full-time employee maternity leave policy but does not have documentation covering contract employee benefits — provide what is known and direct to HR for the unanswered portion
Return no answer since the specific question (contract employee maternity leave) has no matching document
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Clearly stating what is known and what is absent is the correct approach — transparency about knowledge gaps prevents employees from applying the wrong policy.
[Why B fails]: Answering with the full-time policy and adding 'policies may differ' is misleading — the employee may apply the full-time policy to a situation where it doesn't apply.
[Why C fails]: Returning no answer at all wastes the partial information (full-time policy exists) that may still be useful context for the employee.
[Why D fails]: Synthesizing from adjacent documents to fill a gap creates hallucinated policy information — the system should represent actual documented policy, not infer it.
[Principle]: Distinguish between what is documented and what is absent — provide what's known while clearly flagging the gap rather than synthesizing from adjacent content.
[Doc]: RAG – Knowledge Gap Handling → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation
[Doc]: Transparency & Uncertainty → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What chunking strategy best preserves document coherence for this knowledge base?
questions.scenario: Your enterprise knowledge base indexes 3,000 policy documents. Current chunking splits documents every 512 tokens regardless of structure, which causes retrieval to return fragments that lack context — e.g., a retrieved chunk says 'employees are eligible after completing the waiting period' without specifying what the waiting period is (defined in the previous chunk).
options: Add a post-retrieval expansion step that automatically fetches the preceding chunk whenever an incomplete reference is detected

Implement semantic chunking that respects document structure (sections, headings, paragraphs) and includes contextual overlap — each chunk includes a brief header with document name and section title for standalone comprehension
Store the full document and use the full document as the retrieval unit rather than chunks
Increase chunk size from 512 to 2,048 tokens to include more surrounding context
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Structure-aware chunking with contextual headers gives each chunk standalone coherence — retrieved chunks contain enough context to be understood without adjacent chunks.
[Why B fails]: Larger fixed-size chunks reduce but don't eliminate the fragmentation problem — a definition can still be separated from its reference by more than 2,048 tokens.
[Why C fails]: Full-document retrieval injects thousands of tokens per retrieved document, overwhelming the context window for a query that needs 3-4 specific sections.
[Why D fails]: Automated detection of incomplete references is fragile — the system can't reliably know when a chunk is missing context; structural chunking prevents the problem.
[Principle]: Use structure-aware chunking with contextual headers to ensure retrieved chunks are self-contained rather than arbitrary text fragments.
[Doc]: RAG (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: What index maintenance strategy keeps the knowledge base current?
questions.scenario: Your enterprise RAG system indexes 3,000 policy documents. HR updates approximately 50 documents per month (revisions, new policies, deprecations). Currently, the index is rebuilt from scratch weekly. Employees have reported finding outdated answers on freshly-revised policies because revisions published mid-week don't appear until the next Sunday rebuild.
options: Add a 'last updated' timestamp to every response so employees know when the knowledge base was last rebuilt
Implement event-driven incremental indexing triggered by document updates — index updated documents within minutes of publication, with soft-deletes for deprecated documents and version tagging
Reduce the rebuild interval from weekly to daily to shorten the maximum staleness window
For each query, check whether any retrieved document's source has been modified since indexing and re-index it on demand before answering
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Event-driven incremental indexing with soft-deletes and version tagging eliminates staleness for high-priority revisions without the cost of full rebuilds.
[Why B fails]: Daily rebuilds reduce the window from 7 days to 1 day but don't eliminate it — a policy revised at 10am may still be stale until the next rebuild at midnight.
[Why C fails]: Timestamps tell employees when the index was rebuilt, not whether their specific query is affected — it doesn't solve the staleness problem.
[Why D fails]: On-demand re-indexing per query adds significant latency (seconds to minutes for indexing) to what should be a fast retrieval operation.
[Principle]: Use event-driven incremental indexing for time-sensitive document updates rather than scheduled full rebuilds.
[Doc]: RAG (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: How should multi-hop reasoning be implemented in this RAG system?
questions.scenario: An employee asks: 'Am I eligible for the employee stock purchase plan?' The eligibility answer requires: (1) checking employment type from HR systems, (2) finding the ESPP eligibility criteria (requires >6 months tenure AND full-time status), and (3) checking the employee's start date from HR. The current single-pass RAG retrieves the ESPP policy but fails to connect it to the employee's actual eligibility.
options: Answer the question about general ESPP eligibility criteria and ask the employee to verify their own eligibility against the criteria

Implement a multi-step retrieval workflow: first retrieve ESPP eligibility criteria, then use those criteria to determine what employee data to query, then query HR tools for employment type and tenure, and finally synthesize the personalized eligibility answer
Include the employee's full HR profile in every query context so the model always has both policy and personal data available
Retrieve all potentially relevant documents (ESPP policy, employment type definitions, tenure requirements) in a single retrieval pass and ask the model to synthesize eligibility
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Multi-step retrieval — policy criteria first, then targeted HR data queries based on what criteria demand — is the correct architecture for personalized eligibility questions.
[Why B fails]: Including the full HR profile in every query injects potentially hundreds of fields the model doesn't need for most queries, adding cost and noise.
[Why C fails]: Single-pass retrieval of 'all relevant documents' doesn't retrieve the employee's specific tenure and employment type from HR systems, which are in a database, not a document.
[Why D fails]: Asking the employee to self-verify defeats the purpose of a RAG system with HR system integration; the system has the data to provide a definitive answer.
[Principle]: Implement multi-hop retrieval: extract required criteria from policy documents, then query structured systems for the specific data those criteria require.
[Doc]: RAG (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: What access control design is correct for this enterprise knowledge base?
questions.scenario: Your enterprise RAG system serves multiple departments. Some documents are restricted: executive compensation documents (HR+C-suite only), pending merger documents (legal team only), and standard company policies (all employees). The current system retrieves based on semantic relevance only, without access controls.
options: Route restricted queries to a separate secure RAG instance with its own index for sensitive documents
Retrieve all relevant documents and then filter the synthesis results to remove content from restricted documents post-retrieval

Implement pre-retrieval access filtering using the authenticated user's role and department — only index documents the user is authorized to access before semantic search runs, so restricted content is never even retrieved
Add an access control disclaimer to each response: 'You may not have access to all source documents used in this answer'
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Pre-retrieval access filtering ensures restricted documents are never retrieved or exposed, even if their content appears relevant to a query.
[Why B fails]: Post-retrieval filtering may already expose restricted content to the model's context window even if it's removed from the output — the information may still influence the answer.
[Why C fails]: A disclaimer acknowledging potential access gaps doesn't enforce access control — it just warns users of a security gap.
[Why D fails]: Separate RAG instances per sensitivity level adds operational complexity; pre-retrieval filtering is the standard and simpler approach.
[Principle]: Enforce access control before retrieval, not after — restricted content must never enter the model's context window.
[Doc]: Access Control & Permissions → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/permissions
[Doc]: Secure Deployment → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/secure-deployment
[Doc]: Pre-Retrieval Filtering → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation
[Doc]: Security Overview → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/security

questions.question: How should the system handle this query that spans structured and unstructured data?
questions.scenario: An employee asks: 'How much PTO do I have left and what is our carryover policy?' The answer requires: (1) current PTO balance (structured data in HR system, real-time), and (2) PTO carryover policy (unstructured document in knowledge base). The current RAG system can only answer the policy question but not the balance query.
options: Store a snapshot of employee PTO balances in the document index so the system can answer both questions from a unified source

Implement a hybrid retrieval architecture with both document retrieval (for policy) and an HR API tool (for balance) — detect which part of the question requires each source and synthesize the combined answer
Create a dedicated PTO assistant that handles both balance queries and policy questions by combining an HR API integration with a policy subset
Answer only the policy portion from the knowledge base and tell the employee to check the HR portal for their balance
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Hybrid retrieval — document RAG for policy, API tool for real-time structured data — is the correct architecture when questions span both data types.
[Why B fails]: Partial answers with redirects defeat the system's purpose; employees want one place to get their complete question answered.
[Why C fails]: Storing PTO balance snapshots creates stale data — balances change frequently and snapshots would give incorrect information within hours.
[Why D fails]: A dedicated assistant is over-engineered for what's a straightforward addition of a balance lookup tool to the existing system.
[Principle]: Implement hybrid retrieval combining document search with API tools for questions that span unstructured and structured data sources.
[Doc]: RAG – Hybrid Retrieval → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation
[Doc]: MCP Integration (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/mcp
[Doc]: MCP Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp

questions.question: What is the correct handling for this ambiguous query?
questions.scenario: An employee asks your enterprise RAG system: 'What's the policy on gifts?' The knowledge base contains: a gifts-from-vendors policy (ethics/compliance), a holiday gift allowance policy (HR), and a client gifts policy (sales). Semantic search returns all three with similar relevance scores (0.71, 0.68, 0.65).
options: Return a combined synthesis of all three policies with a note that the query matched multiple policy areas
Return the highest-scoring result (gifts-from-vendors at 0.71) and note that other policies may also be relevant
Ask a clarifying question to determine context: 'Are you asking about gifts from vendors, holiday gifts for employees, or gifts to clients?' before retrieving and synthesizing
Return all three policies since the query is ambiguous and the employee may need all of them
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: When a query is ambiguous with multiple equally-valid interpretations, requesting clarification before retrieval is more efficient than synthesizing potentially irrelevant content.
[Why B fails]: Returning all three policies when the employee needs only one creates unnecessary reading burden and potential confusion about which applies to their situation.
[Why C fails]: Returning the highest-scored single result when the scores are near-equal (0.71, 0.68, 0.65) arbitrarily selects from equally likely interpretations.
[Why D fails]: Synthesizing all three into one answer may create apparent contradictions (vendor gift limits vs. client gift allowances) that confuse rather than help.
[Principle]: Clarify ambiguous queries before retrieval when multiple interpretations are equally plausible and equally scored.
[Doc]: Ambiguity Resolution Patterns → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Tool Definitions – Disambiguation → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#best-practices-for-tool-definitions
[Doc]: RAG (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: What is the most reliable way to measure RAG answer quality over time?
questions.scenario: Your enterprise RAG system has been live for 6 months. Management wants to understand if answer quality is improving or degrading over time as the knowledge base grows (now 5,000 documents vs. 500 at launch). You've been logging all queries and responses but haven't analyzed them systematically.
options: Run the same 50 benchmark queries from launch every month and compare answer quality to the baseline

Build a continuous evaluation pipeline: sample 50 queries per week, have subject-matter experts rate answers on accuracy and citation quality, track metrics over time, and set regression alerts when quality drops below threshold
Track retrieval metrics (semantic similarity scores between query and retrieved chunks) as a proxy for answer quality
Measure user satisfaction via thumbs up/down feedback buttons on each response and track the weekly satisfaction rate
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Expert-rated continuous sampling with quantitative metrics and regression alerts provides reliable, systematic quality tracking over time.
[Why B fails]: Thumbs up/down feedback is sparse (low participation), subjective (employees rate helpfulness, not accuracy), and can be gamed or ignored.
[Why C fails]: 50 fixed benchmark queries measure performance on known cases but don't capture the distribution of real queries, especially as new question types emerge with the expanded knowledge base.
[Why D fails]: Retrieval similarity scores measure retrieval quality, not answer quality — high similarity doesn't guarantee the retrieved content was synthesized accurately.
[Principle]: Use expert-rated evaluation samples with quantitative metrics and regression alerts for systematic ongoing quality measurement.
[Doc]: Developing Evaluations → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/test-and-evaluate/develop-tests
